summary: Helps course teams understand, evidence and strengthen their students' graduate futures story through clear course-level evidence packs, a self-run sense-making workshop, shared review and a business-as-usual process that supports ongoing enhancement.
detailSummary:
This deliverable gives King's a clearer and more useful account of graduate futures at course level. It brings together employability outcomes, graduate destinations, benchmarking, student aspiration and readiness data, comparator evidence and course context into evidence packs that academic colleagues can use to understand what their courses make possible for students. Packs are configured at course level and may be distributed together as departmental bundles.

The purpose is to help course teams develop a stronger evidence-informed narrative about their students' graduate futures and identify proportionate actions where the evidence suggests that support could be strengthened. The model combines trusted evidence with a self-run sense-making workshop and a two-sided Graduate Futures Claim and Action on a Page. The approach uses caveats, academic judgement and responsible comparison throughout, avoiding course ranking or central judgement. Course-owned claims can feed wider graduate premium evidence work, while agreed actions enter normal course, department, faculty and quality enhancement processes.
problemSolved: King's holds employability outcomes, destination, benchmarking and student insight data, but course teams do not yet have a consistent way to bring this evidence together, interpret it responsibly and relate it to the support their students receive.
whatChanges: Course teams receive trusted course-level evidence and a self-run workshop that help them claim their graduate futures value with confidence and identify proportionate action. Departments can work with bundles of course packs, while King's gains a coherent view of course-level claims, actions, strengths and support needs.

caseForChange.problem: King's holds employability outcomes, destination, benchmarking and student insight data, but course teams do not yet have a consistent way to bring this evidence together, interpret it responsibly and relate it to the support their students receive. Evidence can therefore remain fragmented, headline-led or difficult to use, limiting both confident articulation of course value and informed decisions about where course-level practice could better support graduate outcomes.
caseForChange.opportunity: Course-level graduate futures data packs, supported by a self-run sense-making workshop, can turn existing evidence into a practical asset for academic teams. Packs can be bundled for departments while retaining course-level insight, helping colleagues identify distinctive strengths, ask better questions, agree proportionate enhancements and develop credible accounts of what their courses enable for students.
caseForChange.whyNow: Graduate outcomes, highly skilled employment and evidence of student value increasingly shape institutional accountability, student choice, TEF narratives, course review and recruitment. At a time of pressure on the graduate premium, declining student numbers and intensifying competition, King's needs a timely, consistent and responsible way for course and department teams to understand their evidence, improve support and communicate value with confidence.
caseForChange.intendedChange: King's moves from fragmented employability reporting to a repeatable course-level model combining trusted evidence with structured local sense-making. Course teams use the packs to articulate the value they already create, identify where students could be better supported to achieve strong graduate outcomes, and agree enhancements through normal course, department and faculty processes. Departments and King's gain a clearer view of recurring strengths, questions and support needs.
